coredata.dc:title: Morinda coreia fruits derived green-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions from water
coredata.dc:creator: Tony Elizabeth, A.
Denis Arockiaraj, S.
Rajasekaran, A.I.
Vasu, Antonisamy Edwin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dot containing nitrogen has been prepared by utilizing a green biomass resource, Morinda coreia fruits, by a single step hydrothermal procedure. The spherical carbon dots are analysed by HR-TEM, XPS, FT-IR, UV–visible and photoluminescence studies. With a mean diameter of 1.99 nm, they possessed functional groups like carboxyl and amide on their surface and emit light of wavelength of 490 nm under 390 nm excitation. The fluorescent Morinda coreia carbon dots (MCCDs), whose quantum yield is found to be 17.1 %, are very sensitive to the presence of iron (III) ions with a low detection limit of 1.32 μM. Successful fluorometric determination of Fe3+ ions from tap water and borewell water were also achieved with good recovery rates. Interactions between surface groups on MCCDs and Fe3+ ions lead to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex, and a static quenching mechanism is proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Static quenching
Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions
Morinda coreia fruits
Quantum yield
Tap water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Morinda coreia fruits
Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and other metal cations
Structural, chemical and optical characterizations of MCCDs
Selective and sensitive fluorescence sensing studies of Fe3+ions
Possible quenching mechanism of MCCDs by Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The corresponding author (Antonisamy Edwin Vasu) gratefully acknowledges the Jesuit Management, St. Joseph's College (Autonomous), Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, India, for providing facilities to carry out research reported in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images ((a) and (b)) and SAED pattern (c) of MCCDs.
Full XPS scan (a), and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d) of MCCDs.
UV–visible spectra with different dilutions (a) and excitation-emission spectra (b) of MCCDs.
PL spectra of MCCDs with different metal cations (a) and effect of Fe3+ concentration on the PL intensity (b).
Effect of pH (a), ionic strength (b) and time (c) on the stability of MCCDs [100-fold diluted in (a) and (b) and 10-fold diluted in (c)].
UV–visible spectra of MCCDs–Fe3+ (250 μM) systems with 20-fold (a) and 200-fold dilution of MCCDs.
LODs of carbon dots from some recent studies toward ferric ions.

row.entry: Seeds of Delonix regia and ethylene diamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of ferric ions in domestic waters by MCCDs.

head.ce:title: Morinda coreia fruits derived green-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions from water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Holy Cross College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Holy Cross College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph’s College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph’s College (Autonomous), Affiliated to Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620 002, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green emissive, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots prepared from Morinda coreia fruits used as fluorescent sensor for the detection of ferric ions from water.

Carbon quantum dot containing nitrogen has been prepared by utilizing a green biomass resource, Morinda coreia fruits, by a single step hydrothermal procedure. The spherical carbon dots are analysed by HR-TEM, XPS, FT-IR, UV–visible and photoluminescence studies. With a mean diameter of 1.99 nm, they possessed functional groups like carboxyl and amide on their surface and emit light of wavelength of 490 nm under 390 nm excitation. The fluorescent Morinda coreia carbon dots (MCCDs), whose quantum yield is found to be 17.1 %, are very sensitive to the presence of iron (III) ions with a low detection limit of 1.32 μM. Successful fluorometric determination of Fe3+ ions from tap water and borewell water were also achieved with good recovery rates. Interactions between surface groups on MCCDs and Fe3+ ions lead to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex, and a static quenching mechanism is proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions are essential components of every living organism and are key players in many biological processes like energy production, oxygen transport, deoxyribonucleic acid replication and repair [1]. But they become toxic at higher concentrations to humans [2,3], plants [4] and also induce chronic toxicity in aquatic organisms [5]. As more and more of iron ions are introduced into the natural water systems by geochemical, microbial and most importantly by the anthropogenic activities [6,7], there is a need to develop methods that are able to detect such ions which are highly accurate and economically viable as well. Though many analytical techniques like AAS, ICP, UV–visible and colorimetric methods are available for the determination of ferric ions in natural waters [8,9], fluorometric detection by carbon quantum dots is emerging as a prominent technique as its sensitivity is high. [10,11].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have zero-dimensional nanostructures, with a carbon core that is primarily built by sp2/sp3 hybridized carbons and a shell surrounding the core which is composed of functional groups like carboxyl and others [12]. They are usually characterized by their very small size (less than 10 nm), facile synthesis, good water solubility, biocompatibility, strong fluorescent character and tuneable optical properties [13]. Because of these properties they find widespread applications in photodynamic and thermal therapy, targeted drug delivery [14], photovoltaics [13], energy applications [15] and as optical sensors in environmental monitoring [16,17]. As many heavy metal ions like Fe3+ have the ability to quench the fluorescence intensity of CQDs by a static quenching effect (SQE) mechanism [18] and others by means of the surface functional groups present on their surface [19a], and since green carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen can be very easily prepared from a variety of chemicals [19b,19c] and cheap plant biomasses [20–22a,22b], it is proposed in this study to prepare CQDs from one such locally available plant biomass which has no commercial value. As the nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) prepared recently from Crescentia cujete fruit waste were very effective in the selective sensing of Fe3+ ions from water [23], it is proposed in this study to make such an attempt with Morinda coreia fruits. Morinda coreia Buch. Ham. Rubiaceae is a small tree belonging to the Rubiaceae family. It is also known as Nuna in Tamil and Yo-paa in Thai and is widely distributed in Southeast Asia [24]. The leaves, roots and fruits have medicinal values, but the fruit is not consumed by humans because of its unpleasant smell [24]. But since the fruits are abundant, the phytochemicals in them can be used for the preparation of nitrogen-doped CQDs. The present work, therefore, aims at the preparation of CQDs from the fruits of Morinda coreia by one-step hydrothermal method, characterize them and to evaluate their applicability as a sensor for the detecting the presence of ferric ions in some selected domestic waters.

Metal cations are derived from their chlorides (Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), Mn(II), Na(I)), sulphates (Fe(II), Mg(II), Ni(II)), acetates (Pb(II), Zn(II)) or nitrate (Ag(I)) that were supplied by Merk India limited. All dilutions were done with double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Morinda coreia fruits
ce:section.ce:para: Morinda coreia fruits collected locally were washed gently with water to remove adhered dust particles. About 3 g of the fruit is placed in a beaker containing 30 mL water, crushed with a glass rod and kept aside for one hour. Pulp and seeds were removed by filtering with ordinary filter paper and 0.22 μm membrane filter was further used to remove fine suspended solid particles from the extract. Bottum-up carbonization of the extract was achieved by hydrothermal reaction in a 50 mL capacity autoclave reactor. After heating for 24 h in a muffle furnace which was kept at 180 °C, the reactor was cooled. Solid residue separated by filtration with 0.22 μm membrane filter was discarded and the MCCDs solution was kept at 5 °C and taken for studies when needed.

Perkin Elmer (Spectrum Two) infrared spectrometer was used to record IR spectra. While a 200 kV HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope was used to image the particles and perform morphological analysis of MCCDs, K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS KAlpha Surface Analysis) supplied by Thermo Fischer Scientific was used to record the XPS spectra. Optical absorption spectra were obtained from Perkin Elmer (Lambda 365) spectrometer and Perkin Elmer (LS45) spectrofluorometer was used to measure photoluminescence (PL) spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and other metal cations
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of different metal cations (trivalent Fe, divalent Ca, Cd, Co, Cu, Fe, Hg, Mg, Mn, Ni, Pb, Zn and monovalent Ag and Na) in altering the fluorescence intensities of MCCDs were evaluated by recording the PL spectra of MCCDs with and without these metal ions. One millilitre of metal ion solutions (500 μM) were added to 1 mL of MCCDs (10-fold diluted), equilibrated for 15 min and then the PL spectra were recorded. An aliquot of 1 mL MCCDs solution with 1 mL of water was used as the blank. For testing the presence of iron in domestic waters, ferric ions were spiked into these waters, mixed with 1 mL of MCCDs solutions (100-fold diluted) such that the final concentrations of iron ions are 20 μM and the PL intensities measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, chemical and optical characterizations of MCCDs
ce:section.ce:para: One-step hydrothermal preparation of nitrogen incorporated carbon dots has been achieved in this study from the fruits of Morinda coreia. High resolution transmission electron microscopic technique was used to do morphological analysis and study the particle size distribution. The results (Fig. 1 a and 1b) show that the nitrogen-doped carbon dots are spherical with almost similar size. The inset in Fig. 1a depicts the particle size distribution for a total number of 100 carbon spheres whose sizes have been measured by imageJ software. A very low average particle size of 1.99 nm was found for 100 spheres. Magnification of a carbon dot cluster (Fig. 1b) reveals the graphitic layers present in them and the interplanar spacing between the (100) planes is clearly seen whose average value was 0.23 nm from 25 measurements made. This interplanar spacing value of 0.23 nm is very close to those of many nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared from plant biomasses [25–27]. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of MCCDs (Fig. 1c) shows diffuse ring patterns with bright spots that are characteristic of poly-nanocrystalline, highly ordered graphitic carbon quantum dots [28–30].

The full XPS scan (Fig. 2 a) of MCCDs shows distinct peaks for the three elements, namely, carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, suggesting that nitrogen has been well doped into the carbon core which might have been originated from the phytochemicals present in the fruit extract. Deconvolution of the C1s peak yielded peaks around 284.7 eV, 285.2 eV and 289.1 eV, which are due to, respectively, sp2/sp3 carbons belonging to the carbon core, C–O and C–N single bonds and carbonyl carbons of carboxylic and amide like functional groups at the surface [31,27]. The O1s peak on deconvolution reveals peaks near 530.6 eV, 531.7 eV and 533.5 eV that correspond to, respectively, oxygens connected to carbons by single bonds, carbonyl oxygens and hydroxy oxygens [32]. Obviously, presence of carboxylic and carbonyl groups is further confirmed from these peaks. Peaks that are centred around 398.8 eV, 400.2 eV and 402.6 eV in N1s spectrum are due to pyrrolic N, graphitic N and amino N functionalities [32,33]. Pyrrolic N centres could have been formed from condensation of carboxylic and amino groups [34] on the surface of growing or grown carbon cores.

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3 ) effectively summarizes the surface composition and nature of functional groups present at the outer surfaces of MCCDs. The strong broad band about 3430 cm−1 is compelling evidence for abundant –COOH and –NH2 functional groups on the surface which are due to the O–H/N–H stretching absorptions [35]. Since these groups, as evidenced by the intensity of this band in the IR spectrum, are abundant on the surface of MCCDs, they are highly dispersible in water and also have the ability to strongly interact with a hard metal ion like ferric as these groups are also hard bases [19a]. Shoulders at 2923 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 stem from aliphatic C–H vibrations [35]. The second most intense band has peaks at about 1700 cm−1 and 1616 cm−1 due to C = O vibrations and olefinic vibrations in the graphitic layer [36]. Carbon-nitrogen single and double bond vibrations are present in the form of shoulder around 1514 cm−1, and peaks around 1440 cm−1 and 1375 cm−1 [37]. Evidences for the C–O connections are found in the form of absorptions ranging from 1020 cm−1 to 1300 cm−1 [38].

UV–visible absorptions of MCCDs with different dilutions are given in Fig. 4 a. Two bands are very clear, namely the strong distinct band around 283 nm (n–π* excitations of the C = O bonds) and the shoulder about 330 nm (n–π* excitations of the C–N bonds). Carbon/graphene quantum dots which lack a heteroatom like nitrogen usually have peaks around 220–230 nm and around 280 nm which are due to the π–π* transitions of the graphitic core and n–π* transitions of the C = O groups [39]. The n–π* transitions of (C = O) are often red-shifted in the case of N-doped carbon dots like MCCDs and they also exhibit an additional peak beyond 300 nm that is due to the n–π* excitations of the C = N bonds [40,41]. Dopping of nitrogen is therefore evident from the shoulder around 330 nm in the UV–visible spectra. Though the absorbances of 17.5 and 20 times diluted MCCD solutions are too high, the presence of the 283 nm band is very clear even at these higher concentrations. Absorptions below 250 nm were very strong and are due to the π–π* transitions of the graphitic core [42,40]. The UV spectrum of 1000 times diluted MCCDs appears to be different with the disappearance of the 283 nm and 330 nm bands which are mainly due to the surface chromophores. Under this extremely diluted state, only the graphitic carbon core was the major absorbing component and the π–π* absorptions of C = C bonds in carbon cores are visible around 208 nm which was unseen clearly as a distinct band thus far. The excitation-emission spectra of MCCDs are given in Fig. 4b which is typical of fluorescing carbon quantum dots. Absorption of 390 nm resulted in the emission of 490 nm wavelength with a Stoke’s shift of 100 nm. Further, excitation wavelength dependency of the PL emission was studied and the results (Fig. S1) show that PL emission intensity was maximum when the excitation was at 390 nm and decreases with increase in wavelength along with a red shift in emission wavelength. Actual surface composition of carbon dots affecting the band gap in them and size effect are responsible for this phenomenon and are common in many fluorescing carbon dots [43,44]. The quantum yield (QY) of MCCDs was evaluated by the relative method wherein 53 % quantum yield was assigned to a solution of quinine sulphate (QS) in sulphuric acid (0.1 M) and making use of Eqn. (1), where φ stand for the quantum yields, η stand for refractive indices, which were assumed to be the same for both MCCDs and QS, A stand for optical absorptions and I stand for the values of integrated fluorescent intensities [45]. The value of QY calculated for MCCDs based on the PL spectra of MCCDs and QS solutions whose absorbance values are less than 0.05 in order to minimize re-absorption effects (Fig. S2) was found to be 17.1 %.
(1)
φ
MCCDs
=
φ
QS
×
η
MCCDs
2
η

2

MCCDs
×

I
QS

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive fluorescence sensing studies of Fe3+ions
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use MCCDs as sensors for Fe3+ ions in real waters, effect of other important parameters like pH, ionic strength and time are to be studied. The effect of these factors on the PL intensity of MCCDs is given in Fig. 6 . The nitrogen-doped carbon dots are highly stable over a wide pH range of 4 to 8 (Fig. 6a) and the PL intensity is maximum around pH 9. Both extremely acidic (<2) and basic (>9) pHs lead to decrease in intensity as this is true with many NCQDs and is mainly due to protonation/deprotonation processes at surface functional groups that cause disruption in surface charges [41]. In addition, the rapid decrease in PL intensity at pHs greater than 9 could be due to the disruption of H-bonding through deprotonation which leads to decrease of vibrational coupling between OH groups on the surface, ultimately resulting in decreased PL intensity [53]. The stability of MCCDs towards ionic strength was tested with NaCl and the results (Fig. 6b) show that they are extremely stable and almost unaffected up to a concentration of 1.5 M and only very slightly incresed beyond 1.5 M concentration of NaCl. This suggests that MCCDs can be successfully used as sensors even with highly saline waters. Finally, the stability of the dots with respect to time were assessed by recording the PL spectra at different times. The PL spectrum recorded after 15 days (Fig. 6c) displays only slightly reduced intensity, implying that MCCDs can be used for longer periods of time by simply perserving at low temperatures. Two domestic real waters, namely, borewell water and tap water were selected to evaluate the potential of MCCDs as sensors for Fe3+. The real waters, without any pretreatment and filtration, were spiked with 20 μM ferric ions, the PL intensities were measured and the recovered amounts are given in Table 2 . The recovery rates are fairly good and portray the sensitivity of MCCDs towards ferric ions in them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of MCCDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Quenching of fluorescence of carbon dots by metal ions can be explained mainly through one or more of three major mechanisms which are inner filter effect, static quenching and dynamic quenching [54]. Since aqueous Fe3+ has no significant absorptions at both the excitation (390 nm) and emission wavelengths (490 nm) of MCCDs (Fig. 4b and Fig. 7 ), possiblity of inner filter effect mechanism is ruled out [55]. Therefore, the operative mechanism can be static quenching or dynamic quenching or a combination of both. Application of Stern-Volmer equation (Eqn. (2)) is a useful guide to decide in such a case, where F and F0 are the fluorescent intensities of MCCDs with and without the quencher (Fe3+), KS–V is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Fe3+] is iron ions concentration.
(2)
F
F 0
= 1 +
K
S - V
Fe
3 +

Plot of F0/F against Fe3+ concentration is perfectly linear from 0 to 250 μM concentration range with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9990 (Fig. S7). It is inferred, therefore, that either static or dynamic quenching mechanism is operative at the system in hand and joint operation of the two mechanisms is eliminated [55,56]. The Stern-Volmer constant was found to be 6.3 × 103 M−1 and this high value is an indication of very strong interactions prevailing in the MCCDs–Fe3+ system and signals involvement of a stable adduct between the sensor and quencher which is not fluorescing and therefore, involvemnet of a static quenching mechanism [57]. Further investigation was done by recording the UV–visible spectra of MCCDs of 20-fold and 200-fold diluted solutions with and without Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7). The UV–visible spectra of 20-times diluted MCCDs very clearly show their characteristic peak at 283 nm though the absorbance is extremely high (Fig. 7a). Intensities of this peak decreased gradually and the band is very clearly seen with 150 μM Fe3+ and other higher concentrations. While the intensity of the 283 nm band decreased with the addition of ferric ions, a shoulder appears around 365 nm which is missing in the UV spectra of pure MCCDs and this could be due to the formation of its complex with ferric ions. Very similar results are also realized with 200-times diluted MCCDs solutions (Fig. 7b). After the addtion of 250 μM Fe3+ the 283 nm peak weakened and the shoulder around 365 appeared. It is to be noted that though absorption of Fe3+ is higher than 200-fold diluted MCCDs and an inner filter mechanism is anticipated from Fig. 7b, spectral changes with higher concentrations as in Fig. 7a point that complex formation is significant. These changes in the absorption spectra suggest strong interactions beween the carboxyl and amino functionalities of MCCDs with Fe3+ ions and the operation of static quenching mechanism [56]. To confirm the complex formation, FT-IR spectrum of MCCDs–Fe3+ system is recoreded and is compared with that of pure MCCDs (Fig. S8). Reduction in the intensities of O–H/N–H bands and C–O/C–N vibrations are observed in the spectrum with Fe3+, confirming the role of surface groups in complexation. As these groups coordinatively interact with Fe3+ ions, their peak intensities are much reduced. Further evidence for the same is apparent from the appearance of a new moderate band which has absorptions around 460 cm−1, 530 cm−1, and 600 cm−1 that could possibly be absorptions by the newly developed bonding interactions established between iron and oxygen centres. Based on the above observations, it can be concluded that complexation occurs between surface groups of carbon dots and the quencher, and after the absorption of light by MCCDs in the non-fluorescent complex, excited state electrons are transferred through the surface groups in to the vaccant d-orbitals of Fe3+. This leads to a non-radiative electron-hole recombination and could possibly be the reason for the fluorescene quenching [58].

A green preparative method for nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots has been achieved in this work using waste Morinda coreia fruits as the carbon resource in a bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis. The morphology and chemical composition of green fluorescing carbon dots have been systematically done by such techniques as HR-TEM, XPS and FT-IR. The average diameter of the spherial dots has been found to be 1.99 nm. Considerable amounts of –COOH, and –CONH2 functionalities are attached at the outer shell of these particles by means of which they are able to exhibit a fair quantum yield value of 17.1 % and strongly bind with Fe3+ ions. The very high sensitivity of MCCDs towards ferric ions is evident from a relatively large KS–V value of 6.3 × 103 M−1 and PL studies reveal that the limit of detection is 1.32 μM. Apart from being able to detect ferric ions from ideal water, MCCDs are also found to be very effective in the detection of them from two real waters like borewell water and tap water with good recovery rates. Non-fluorescing complex formed between dots and quencher leads to the transfer of excited state electrons into the d orbital of iron and the fluorescence reduction is through a static quenching pathway.

A. Tony Elizabeth: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. S. Denis Arockiaraj: Methodology, Formal analysis. A.I. Rajasekaran: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Antonisamy Edwin Vasu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The corresponding author (Antonisamy Edwin Vasu) gratefully acknowledges the Jesuit Management, St. Joseph’s College (Autonomous), Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, India, for providing facilities to carry out research reported in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.112390.
